sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: Three analgesic standards were separated by thin-layer chromatography on polar silica using a moderately nonpolar solvent. Compound A had Rf = 0.20, B had Rf = 0.48, and C had Rf = 0.72. A mixture contained spots at Rf 0.20 and 0.72. Students inferred relative polarity from interactions with the stationary phase.
questions.stem: Which compound is most polar based on the TLC data?

explanations.C: Correct. On polar silica, more polar compounds interact more strongly with the stationary phase and travel less far, giving lower Rf values. Compound A has the lowest Rf at 0.20.
explanations.A: Compound B has an intermediate Rf and therefore intermediate retention.
explanations.B: Compound C traveled farthest and is least retained by polar silica.
explanations.D: The solvent front is not one of the compounds being compared.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Thin-layer chromatography Rf values for three compounds on polar silica.

questions.stem: Which standards are present in the mixture?

explanations.B: Correct. The mixture has spots at Rf 0.20 and 0.72, matching the standards for compounds A and C. No spot is reported at B’s Rf of 0.48.
explanations.A: A and B would require spots at 0.20 and 0.48.
explanations.C: B and C would require spots at 0.48 and 0.72.
explanations.D: Only B would produce a single spot around 0.48.

questions.stem: What would happen if a more polar mobile phase were used?

choices.A: Rf values would all become zero
choices.B: Polar compounds would bind silica permanently
choices.C: The stationary phase would become nonpolar

explanations.D: Correct. A more polar solvent competes more effectively with analytes for silica interactions, carrying compounds farther up the plate. Rf values generally increase.
explanations.A: Rf values of zero would imply no movement, opposite of a stronger eluent.
explanations.B: Polar compounds may still be retained more than nonpolar ones but not permanently bound under normal TLC conditions.
explanations.C: Changing solvent does not change silica into a nonpolar stationary phase.

questions.stem: Why does silica retain polar compounds strongly?

choices.A: Silica can form polar interactions and hydrogen bonds
choices.B: Silica is a strong reducing agent

explanations.A: Correct. Silica gel has polar surface groups that can interact with polar functional groups through dipole interactions and hydrogen bonding, slowing polar compounds.
explanations.B: Redox chemistry is not the basis of TLC retention here.
explanations.C: Ionizing radiation is irrelevant to silica TLC separation.
explanations.D: The separation depends on surface interactions.

questions.stem: If two compounds have identical Rf values in this system, what is the best next step?

choices.D: Run another solvent system or analytical method
choices.A: Assume they are definitely the same compound

explanations.D: Correct. Identical Rf in one TLC system is not definitive identification. A different solvent system, co-spotting, melting point, or spectroscopy would provide stronger evidence.
explanations.A: Different compounds can share an Rf under one condition.
explanations.B: There is no stationary phase law to discard; the method simply has limited resolution.
explanations.C: Rf values do not imply the compounds are gases.

questions.passage: An unknown organic compound had a strong IR absorption near 1715 cm−1, no broad absorption near 3200–3600 cm−1, and C–H stretches near 2900 cm−1. In a separate UV-visible experiment, absorbance at 500 nm increased linearly with concentration from 0.10 to 0.50 mM. Students used IR functional-group information and Beer-Lambert behavior.
questions.stem: The strong absorption near 1715 cm−1 most likely indicates:

explanations.C: Correct. A strong band near 1715 cm−1 is characteristic of a C=O stretch. The absence of a broad 3200–3600 cm−1 band argues against an alcohol O–H group.
explanations.A: Alcohol O–H stretches are broad and typically appear around 3200–3600 cm−1.
explanations.B: N–H stretches appear at higher wavenumber and do not explain the strong 1715 cm−1 band.
explanations.D: C≡N stretches usually occur near 2200 cm−1, not 1715 cm−1.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Beer-Lambert calibration curve for the unknown compound at 500 nm.

questions.stem: Which law explains the linear absorbance-concentration relationship?

explanations.B: Correct. Beer-Lambert law states A = εlc, so absorbance is proportional to concentration when path length and molar absorptivity are constant. The plotted linear calibration matches this relationship.
explanations.A: Henderson-Hasselbalch relates pH to acid-base ratios.
explanations.C: Poiseuille’s law describes laminar fluid flow.
explanations.D: Boyle’s law relates pressure and volume for gases.

questions.stem: If absorbance is 0.36, what concentration is estimated from the curve?

explanations.A: Correct. The calibration curve gives A = 0.36 at 0.30 mM. The linear relationship allows direct interpolation from the plotted standards.
explanations.D: 3.0 mM is outside the plotted range and would give much larger absorbance if linearity continued.

questions.stem: The absence of a broad 3200–3600 cm−1 peak argues against:

explanations.C: Correct. Alcohol O–H stretches are broad in the 3200–3600 cm−1 region. Because that feature is absent, an alcohol is less likely.
explanations.A: A ketone carbonyl would be consistent with the 1715 cm−1 absorption.
explanations.B: Alkyl C–H stretches near 2900 cm−1 were observed.
explanations.D: The carbonyl absorption is present, not absent.

questions.stem: Why should very high concentrations sometimes be diluted before UV-visible analysis?

choices.B: Absorbance may leave the linear range of Beer-Lambert behavior

explanations.B: Correct. Beer-Lambert law is most reliable within a linear absorbance range. At very high absorbance, instrumental and chemical deviations can make concentration estimates inaccurate, so dilution improves measurement reliability.
explanations.A: Molar absorptivity does not become exactly zero at high concentration.
explanations.C: Light still interacts with molecules; the issue is linearity and measurement accuracy.
